items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.q: What did Renaissance artists rediscover that medieval art lacked?
items.correct: Linear perspective and naturalistic representation of the human body.
items.why: Students assume medieval art was colourless. It was vivid but flat - the Renaissance breakthrough was mathematical PERSPECTIVE (Brunelleschi) and anatomical realism, not the introduction of colour.

items.q: What is the rule of thirds?
items.correct: A guideline placing key elements along lines or intersections of a 3x3 grid.
items.misconception: A strict mathematical rule that must never be broken.
items.why: Students treat it as inviolable. It's a useful HEURISTIC for engaging compositions - many great works ignore it. Centred compositions can be powerful too; the rule is a starting point, not a law.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Dots. “Lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 colours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
